sources.code:
# Time:  O(n * glogg), g is the max size of groups.
# Space: O(n)

import collections


class Solution(object):
    def groupAnagrams(self, strs):
        """
        :type strs: List[str]
        :rtype: List[List[str]]
        """
        anagrams_map, result = collections.defaultdict(list), []
        for s in strs:
            sorted_str = ("").join(sorted(s))
            anagrams_map[sorted_str].append(s)
        for anagram in anagrams_map.values():
            anagram.sort()
            result.append(anagram)
        return result
sources.originalCode:
# Time:  O(n * glogg), g is the max size of groups.
# Space: O(n)

import collections


class Solution(object):
    def groupAnagrams(self, strs):
        """
        :type strs: List[str]
        :rtype: List[List[str]]
        """
        anagrams_map, result = collections.defaultdict(list), []
        for s in strs:
            sorted_str = ("").join(sorted(s))
            anagrams_map[sorted_str].append(s)
        for anagram in anagrams_map.values():
            anagram.sort()
            result.append(anagram)
        return result
sources.reviewedCode:
# Time:  O(n * glogg), g is the max size of groups.
# Space: O(n)

import collections


# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: If equivalent inputs can share one signature, grouping becomes much simpler.
class Solution(object):
    def groupAnagrams(self, strs):
        """
        :type strs: List[str]
        :rtype: List[List[str]]
        """
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Canonical representations, hash-based grouping, and thinking about data normalization before implementation.
        anagrams_map, result = collections.defaultdict(list), []
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        for s in strs:
            sorted_str = ("").join(sorted(s))
            anagrams_map[sorted_str].append(s)
        for anagram in anagrams_map.values():
            anagram.sort()
            result.append(anagram)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return result

sources.reviewedCode:
# [Reader note] Entry point
# Start from the core routine and keep this mental model in view: If equivalent inputs can share one signature, grouping becomes much simpler.
class Solution:
    def groupAnagrams(self, strs: List[str]) -> List[List[str]]:
        # [Reader note] State setup
        # This setup stage creates the state needed for the invariant or partial result: Canonical representations, hash-based grouping, and thinking about data normalization before implementation.
        d = defaultdict(list)
        # [Reader note] Main pass
        # This is the main sweep where the implementation earns its efficiency instead of restarting work from scratch.
        for s in strs:
            k = ''.join(sorted(s))
            d[k].append(s)
        # [Reader note] Return
        # The final return exposes the result after the traversal and state updates have settled.
        return list(d.values())

writeups.title: 049.Group-Anagrams
writeups.excerpt: ### 049.Group-Anagrams 对每个string按从小到大排序，以此为key建立Hash表 map<string,vector<string>>。属于同一组Anagrams的字符串必定有相同的键值。 [Leetcode Link](https://leetcode.com/problems/group-anagrams)
